lines.target: As befits its status as the capital city, London has had a vital influence on the English language. (N 3)
lines.base: Como rezan sus estatutos de ciudad capital, Londres ha tenido una influencia crucial sobre el idioma inglés.

lines.target: In fact, it was the London dialect that formed the basis of the English language as we know it today.
lines.base: De hecho, fue el dialecto de Londres el que formó las bases de la lengua inglesa tal y como la conocemos en el presente.

lines.target: The influence of Caxton and his printing press meant that the language of the Londoners spread all over the country — and the world.
lines.base: La influencia de Caxton y su prensa de tipografía dio por resultado que la lengua de los londinenses se extendió por todo el país — y el mundo.

lines.target: Nowadays, there are so many ethnic groups which have set up home in London that it is hard to say who or what makes up a real Londoner.
lines.base: En nuestros días, existen tantos grupos étnicos que se han establecido en Londres que es difícil decir quién o qué constituye un verdadero londinense.

lines.target: But let's look at a group of people who are usually identified as being "typical" — the Cockneys.
lines.base: Pero examinemos a un grupo de gentes que se identifica habitualmente como «típico» — los Cockneys.

lines.target: "I get a funny feeling inside of me, just walking up and down
lines.base: «Tengo una sensación extraña en mi interior, con sólo pasearme por aquí, por allá,

lines.target: maybe it's because I'm a Londoner that I love London town."
lines.base: quizás sea debido a que soy un londinense que adora la ciudad de Londres».

lines.target: A true Cockney is someone who is born within the sound of Bow Bells, but we traditionally identify the east end of the city with the Cockneys.
lines.base: Un verdadero Cockney es aquel que ha nacido dentro del radio de acción de Bow Bells (las campanas de Bow), pero identificamos tradicionalmente el distrito Este de la ciudad con los Cockneys.

lines.target: Originally tradesmen, their traditional ceremonial dress is covered with pearl buttons;
lines.base: Mercaderes de origen, sus vestidos tradicionales de ceremonias están cubiertos de botones de nácar;

lines.target: each year, the man and the woman with the finest dress are named Pearly King and Queen.
lines.base: cada año, el hombre y la mujer, con las más hermosas vestimentas son elegidos Rey y Reina del Nácar.

lines.target: Cockneys are renowned for their wit,
lines.base: Los Cockneys son célebres por su ingenio,

lines.target: their repartee — and their fast-talking idiomatic speech called 'rhyming slang'.
lines.base: sus réplicas — y su hablar rápido e idiomático al que llaman «rhyming slang» (argot rimado).

lines.target: Their accent and the speed at which they speak not only bewilder foreigners — people from other parts of Britain are often lost for words.
lines.base: Su acento y la rapidez con que ellos hablan no solamente dejan perplejos a los extranjeros — sino también a gentes de otras partes de la Gran Bretaña que a menudo se pierden con las palabras.

lines.target: For a Cockney, "to die" and "today" have the same sound. Listen: — I don't want to die today.
lines.base: Ya que para un Cockney, «to die» (morir) y «today» (hoy) tiene el mismo sonido: Escuche — Yo no quiero morir hoy.

lines.target: The letter "h" is not pronounced in front of a word, a trait which is known as "dropping one's aitches".
lines.base: La letra «h» no se pronuncia delante de las palabras, una característica que es conocida como «supresión de la h».

lines.target: The letter "g" disappears from the "-ing" form of the verb: — He's having a blooming good holiday,
lines.base: La letra «g» desaparece de la forma «ing» del verbo: — Él pasa unas estupendas vacaciones,

lines.target: would be written down as — 'Es 'avin' a bloomin' good 'oliday.
lines.base: se escribiría como — «Es 'havin' a bloomin' good 'oliday.

lines.target: The influence of the capital is still felt all over the country, as new trends always spread from the centre outwards.
lines.base: La influencia de la capital se hace sentir en todas las demás partes del país, ya que las nuevas tendencias siempre evolucionan del centro hacia afuera.

lines.target: A Cockney asked his doctor — who had an impeccably correct accent — What shall I do for an 'orrible 'eadache?
lines.base: Un Cockney le pidió a su médico — quien tenía un acento impecablemente correcto — ¿Qué puedo hacer con un dolor de cabeza atroz? (NDLR: sin pronunciar la h).

lines.target: The doctor replied — Try a couple of aspirates.
lines.base: El médico respondió — Intente dos o tres «aspiradas».

lines.target: As to the vocabulary, try to understand the speaker who says — in broadest Cockney —
lines.base: En cuanto al vocabulario, trate de comprender al locutor que dice, con un acento Cockney muy exagerado,

lines.target: If you're goin' up the apples and pears with a cup of Rosie Lee, don't spill it on your whistle and flute.
lines.base: Si subes las manzanas y las peras, con una taza de Rosie Lee, no la derrames sobre tu silbato y flauta (NDLR: !).

lines.target: Well, we agree that it is difficult — after all, rhyming slang was developed so that tradesmen could talk together without being understood.
lines.base: Bien estamos de acuerdo que es difícil — después de todo, el «argot rimado» fue elaborado de manera que los mercaderes pudiesen intercambiar sin ser comprendidos.

lines.target: But take the word "stairs"; find a word that rhymes with it: "pears";
lines.base: Pero tomemos la palabra «stairs» (escalera); encuentre una palabra que rime con ella: «pears» (peras);

lines.target: now find a word that goes with the sense of the rhyme: "apples".
lines.base: ahora encuentre una palabra que vaya con el sentido de la rima: «apples» (manzanas).

lines.target: So "apples and pears" means "stairs". Our sentence means:
lines.base: Así «apples and pears» quiere decir «stairs». Nuestra frase significa:

lines.target: If you're going up the stairs ('apples and pears') with a cup of tea ("Rosie Lee"),
lines.base: Si usted sube las escaleras con una taza de té, («Rosie Lee»)

lines.target: don't spill it on your suit ("whistle and flute").
lines.base: no la derrame sobre su traje («whistle and flute»).

gap_candidates.target: As befits its status as the capital city, London has had a vital influence on the English language. (1) (N 3)
But let's look at a group of people who are usually identified as being "typical" — the Cockneys.
A true Cockney is someone who is born within the sound of Bow Bells, but we traditionally identify the east end of the city with the Cockneys. (5)
Originally tradesmen, their traditional ceremonial dress is covered with pearl buttons;
each year, the man and the woman with the finest dress are named Pearly King and Queen.
Cockneys are renowned for their wit,
their repartee — and their fast-talking idiomatic speech called 'rhyming slang'. (6)
Their accent and the speed at which they speak not only bewilder foreigners — people from other parts of Britain are often lost for words. (7)
For a Cockney, "to die" and "today" have the same sound. Listen: — I don't want to die today. (8)
The letter "h" is not pronounced in front of a word, a trait which is known as "dropping one's aitches".
A Cockney asked his doctor — who had an impeccably correct accent — What shall I do for an 'orrible 'eadache?
The doctor replied — Try a couple of aspirates. (9)
As to the vocabulary, try to understand the speaker who says — in broadest Cockney — (10)
If you're goin' up the apples and pears with a cup of Rosie Lee, don't spill it on your whistle and flute.
Well, we agree that it is difficult — after all, rhyming slang was developed so that tradesmen could talk together without being understood. (11)
But take the word "stairs"; find a word that rhymes with it: "pears";
now find a word that goes with the sense of the rhyme: "apples".
If you're going up the stairs ('apples and pears') with a cup of tea ("Rosie Lee"),
